text: More than two-fifths of marathon runners experience severe and performance-limiting depletion of carbohydrate reserves ('hitting the wall') during the race.
Approximately 1-2% of marathon starters drop out before reaching the finish line.
The distance at which a runner exhausts glycogen stores is a predictable function of running intensity (fraction of VO2max), muscle mass distribution, and liver and muscle glycogen densities.
Maximal glycogen loading is not required to complete a marathon without hitting the wall; personally appropriate pacing and midrace fueling can prevent glycogen depletion.
Running at a lower fraction of aerobic capacity (lower exercise intensity) extends the distance before glycogen depletion occurs, making pace selection critical for marathon completion.
Midrace fueling requirements to avoid hitting the wall can be quantified on a personalized basis using individual physiologic variables including glycogen density, muscle mass, and target pace.
